title: Prolactinoma
oneLiner: Benign pituitary lactotroph adenoma causing hyperprolactinemia → hypogonadism, galactorrhea, and mass effects; commonest pituitary tumour, readily treatable.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette**
A 28-year-old woman presents with 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea (previously regular cycles). She mentions spontaneous milky discharge from both breasts when she squeezes them, and she and her partner have been trying for a baby for over a year. She has no significant past medical history, takes no regular drugs (specifically not antipsychotics or antiemetics) and has normal thyroid function. She does not complain of headache or visual disturbance. On examination, BMI is 27 kg/m² and there is bilateral galactorrhea expressible but no other focal neurology.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Secondary amenorrhoea | Hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis disturbance → think hyperprolactinemia as top cause in young woman |
| Bilateral galactorrhea | Prolactin directly stimulates breast tissue; non-puerperal = pathological unless nipple stimulation |
| Infertility | Disrupted GnRH pulsatility → anovulation |
| No drug history | Rules out dopamine-antagonist induced hyperprolactinemia |
| Normal TSH | Rules out hypothyroidism as cause |
| No headache/visual symptoms | Suggests microadenoma (<1 cm) – macroadenomas often present with mass effect |
| Age/sex | Women of reproductive age = peak incidence; microadenomas more common in women |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**

- **Postmenopausal woman** – Found on incidental brain imaging (e.g., for dizziness). No menstrual clue. Galactorrhea may be absent because oestrogen ‘priming’ is low. May present with mass effect (bitemporal hemianopia) or hypopituitarism.
- **Man with erectile dysfunction and low libido** – Often underdiagnosed because men rarely check for galactorrhea. Prolactin suppresses LH/FSH → hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Testosterone low, SHBG low, prolactin high.
- **Child with headache and delayed puberty** – Rare, but consider if growth arrest + visual field defect. Prolactin may be only mildly elevated unless large tumour.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Prolactinoma is a benign (WHO grade I) adenoma arising from lactotroph cells of the anterior pituitary, secreting prolactin in excess. It ranges from microadenomas (<1 cm, usually intrasellar) to macroadenomas (≥1 cm, possibly suprasellar extension).

**Why it matters clinically**

- **Most common pituitary adenoma** (40-60% of all) – incidence ~10 per million per year.
- **Highly treatable** with dopamine agonists; response rates >90% for microprolactinomas. Surgery rarely needed.
- **Missed leads to preventable morbidity** – infertility, osteoporosis due to prolonged hypogonadism, and in macroadenomas: visual loss (irreversible), hypopituitarism, and rarely pituitary apoplexy.
- **Common pitfall:** assuming all hyperprolactinemia is adenoma – up to 40% is drug-induced, hypothyroid, or due to stalk compression from non-prolactin-secreting mass.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
The story begins in the hypothalamus. Dopamine, released from tuberoinfundibular neurons, travels via the hypothalamic-pituitary portal system to the anterior pituitary, where it binds D2 receptors on lactotrophs and **tonically inhibits** prolactin release. Prolactinoma cells lose this inhibitory regulation because they are monoclonal neoplasms with altered D2-receptor signalling.

**Key mechanism → sign chain**

| Mechanism | Resulting sign |
|-----------|----------------|
| Excess prolactin → inhibition of GnRH pulsatility | **Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism** – amenorrhoea, anovulation, erectile dysfunction |
| Loss of GnRH → low LH/FSH → low oestrogen/testosterone | **Osteoporosis** (chronic), **infertility**, **loss of libido** |
| Direct stimulatory effect of prolactin on breast alveolar epithelium | **Galactorrhea** (requires oestrogen priming – more common in premenopausal women) |
| Enlarging adenoma pushes against normal pituitary tissue | **Hypopituitarism** (especially GH, gonadotropins first) |
| Upward growth compresses optic chiasm (most commonly crossing fibres from nasal retinae) | **Bitemporal hemianopia** (early sign: superior quadrantanopia) |
| Lateral extension into cavernous sinus | **Cranial nerve palsies** (III, IV, VI, V1 – rare unless aggressive) |
| Stalk compression by tumour (even if non-secreting) interrupts dopamine flow | **Stalk effect** – hyperprolactinemia from loss of tonic inhibition, often mild (<200 ng/mL) |

**Why pattern recognition matters:** A woman with amenorrhea and galactorrhea – prolactinoma is likely if prolactin >200 ng/mL (tumour) vs <200 ng/mL (stalk effect or other cause). The tumour size loosely correlates with prolactin level: micros usually 200-500, macros >500-1000. Very high levels (>5000) suggest giant or aggressive prolactinoma or malignant transformation.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
**Bin 1 – Idiopathic / spontaneous**
- Most common (~60% of all hyperprolactinemia is not a tumour – stress, nipple stimulation, exercise, sleep, pregnancy). For prolactinoma specifically, ~90% are sporadic.
- **Stem clue:** young woman, no drugs, normal thyroid, prolactin 50-200 ng/mL → often idiopathic hyperprolactinemia (may still receive low-dose agonist if symptomatic).

**Bin 2 – Pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma)**
- Micro (<1 cm) vs macro (≥1 cm)
- **Stem clue:** prolactin >200 ng/mL; degree correlates with tumour size. No other pituitary abnormality on MRI.

**Bin 3 – Drug-induced (functional hyperprolactinemia)**
- **Antipsychotics** (typical >> atypical, but risperidone/paliperidone high risk) – block D2 receptors.
- **Antiemetics** – metoclopramide, domperidone (dopamine antagonists).
- **Others**: verapamil, methyldopa, oral contraceptives (weak), SSRIs (mild).
- **Stem clue:** recent medication start. Withdrawal normalizes prolactin within days-weeks.

**Bin 4 – Hypothalamic-pituitary stalk disorders (“disconnection hyperprolactinemia”)**
- *Stalk compression* by non-prolactin-secreting pituitary mass (non-functioning adenoma, craniopharyngioma, meningioma, Rathke’s cleft cyst) or granulomatous disease (sarcoidosis, histiocytosis)
- **Stem clue:** prolactin <200 ng/mL (usually <150); mass on MRI not arising from sella alone.

**Bin 5 – Hypothyroidism**
- Loss of negative feedback → TRH increase → TRH stimulates lactotrophs as well as thyrotrophs.
- **Stem clue:** elevated TSH, low free T4, prolactin mildly elevated. Replacement of thyroxine normalizes prolactin.

**Bin 6 – Renal failure**
- Decreased clearance of prolactin.
- **Stem clue:** Cr >300, on dialysis. Prolactin usually <200.

**Bin 7 – Miscellaneous**
- Chest wall trauma/surgery/shingles (neural reflex), nipple piercing.
- **Stem clue:** history of chest wall irritation, prolactin mildly high.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria (no formal score – clinical + lab + imaging)**
- **Confirmed hyperprolactinemia** on at least two occasions (>30 ng/mL in women, >20 ng/mL in men) after excluding drugs, hypothyroidism, renal failure, pregnancy.
- **MRI pituitary with contrast** showing a pituitary adenoma (micro or macro).
- **Visual field perimetry** if macroadenoma abutting chiasm.

**Individual tests**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Serum prolactin** | Direct measure | First line | >200 ng/mL strongly suggests prolactinoma; >500 is diagnostic of macroprolactinoma. If very high (>10,000) suspect aggressive/malignant. |
| **Macroprolactin screen** (PEG precipitation) | Detects macroprolactin (big prolactin-immunoglobulin complex) – biologically inactive | If prolactin elevated but patient has no symptoms of hyperprolactinemia (especially men/older women) | True prolactin <30% of total = macroprolactinemia (no treatment needed) |
| **TSH, free T4** | Thyroid function | Exclude hypothyroidism as cause | Elevated TSH + low free T4 → primary hypothyroidism |
| **LH, FSH, oestradiol/testosterone** | Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism | Confirm gonadal insufficiency | Low LH/FSH + low sex steroids |
| **MRI pituitary (sella protocol)** | Tumour size, extent, stalk, chiasm, cavernous sinus | Gold standard imaging | Micro (<1 cm) vs macro (≥1 cm). Look for cystic components, haemorrhage (apoplexy). |
| **Visual field perimetry** | Visual field defects (early superior bitemporal quadrantanopia) | If macroadenoma or any visual complaint | Repeat every 6-12 months until stable |
| **Pituitary function panel** (cortisol, ACTH, IGF-1, etc.) | Rule out hypopituitarism | Especially if macro; baseline before treatment | If cortisol low → needs hydrocortisone; GH deficiency may not need replacement |

**Single finding that confirms / rules out**

- **Confirms macroprolactinoma:** MRI shows ≥1 cm pituitary tumour + prolactin >500 ng/mL.
- **Rules out prolactinoma:** Normal MRI with prolactin <200 ng/mL and no other cause (then idiopathic hyperprolactinemia or microprolactinoma if MRI not sensitive). Very low prolactin (<100) in presence of pituitary mass = likely stalk effect (non-prolactinoma mass).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – if visual compromise or apoplexy**
- **What:** Urgent MRI, ophthalmology review, start high-dose dexamethasone 4 mg IV stat (to reduce oedema) and arrange neurosurgical consultation.
- **Why:** Rapid tumor expansion can cause irreversible vision loss if not decompressed urgently.
- **NOT:** Do not start dopamine agonist if there is apoplexy with hypotension (risk of vasodilation). First, stabilize haemodynamics, give steroids, then plan surgery.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – for stable patients**

- **First-line medical therapy:** **Cabergoline** – a long-acting dopamine D2-receptor agonist.
  - Dose: 0.25-0.5 mg twice weekly orally (start 0.25 mg twice weekly, increase after 4 weeks based on prolactin).
  - Why: Normalizes prolactin in >90%, shrinks tumours, less GI side effects than bromocriptine.
  - Monitor prolactin monthly until normal, then every 6-12 months. MRI at 6 months to assess tumour shrinkage.
- **Second-line:** **Bromocriptine** 2.5-5 mg daily (start 1.25 mg at bedtime, increase over weeks) – only if cabergoline not tolerated or unavailable.
- **Supportive:** Reassure patient, discuss side effects (nausea, postural hypotension, dizziness, rarely impulse control disorders). Give with food, take at bedtime.

- **Check:** Baseline prolactin, MRI, visual fields if macroadenoma. Rule out pregnancy (β-hCG) before starting agonist.

- **What NOT to do:** Don’t give dopamine agonists to patients with psychogenic causes (e.g., antipsychotic-induced) unless symptomatic – usually withdraw the offending drug if safe. Don’t start agonist without confirming no macroprolactinemia.

**Long-term (months-years)**

- **Goal:** Normal prolactin, restored gonadal function, tumour shrinkage.
- **Maintenance cabergoline:** Continue at lowest effective dose. Re-evaluate every 6-12 months.
- **Taper after 2-3 years** if prolactin remains normal and MRI shows no tumour. Attempt to stop drug entirely in 1 in 20 patients (success depends on size, original prolactin). Resumes if prolactin rises.
- **Surgery (transsphenoidal adenomectomy):** Reserved for:
  - Drug intolerance or resistance (failure to normalize prolactin after maximum dose 3 months)
  - Macroprolactinoma with mass effect not responding
  - Apoplexy or CSF leak
  - Cystic prolactinomas (surgery better)
- **Radiotherapy:** Rare – only for aggressive, malignant, or recurrent tumors after surgery and drug failure.

**Common pitfalls**

- **Not checking macroprolactin** in asymptomatic patients – leads to unnecessary treatment.
- **Starting agonist without checking TSH/free T4** – missed hypothyroidism.
- **Not asking about antipsychotics** – classic exam trick.
- **Assuming all pituitary mass is prolactinoma** – stalk effect from non-functioning adenoma will have mildly elevated prolactin (<200) and no tumour shrinkage with dopamine agonist. If prolactin does not normalize or mass does not shrink, consider biopsy.
- **Forgetting pregnancy counseling** – cabergoline is safe in early pregnancy, but stop as soon as pregnancy confirmed; monitor for tumour enlargement (risk 3% for micro, 30% for macro).
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Prolactinoma | Hypothyroidism (primary) | Drug-induced | Stalk effect (non-functioning mass) |
|---------|--------------|--------------------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Prolactin** | >200 ng/mL (micro), >500 (macro) | Mildly elevated (<200) | Mild-mod elevated (<200) | Mildly elevated (<200) |
| **TSH** | Normal | High | Normal | Normal |
| **Free T4** | Normal | Low | Normal | Normal |
| **LH/FSH** | Low (hypogonadotropic) | Normal or low | Variable | Low if mass compresses gonadotrophs |
| **Galactorrhea** | Common (if premenopausal) | Uncommon | Possible | Rare |
| **MRI** | Sellar adenoma (micro/macro) | Normal | Normal | Non-prolactin-secreting mass (stalk compression) |
| **Drug history** | None | None | Haloperidol/risperidone/metoclopramide | None |
| **Key distinguishing test** | Prolactin level + MRI | TSH + free T4 | Withdrawal of drug normalizes prolactin | MRI shows stalk deviation; prolactin not >200 |
| **Management** | Dopamine agonist | Levothyroxine | Stop/replace drug | Surgery if mass effect; dopamine agonist won’t shrink mass |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**If untreated or diagnosed late**

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Osteoporosis** (due to prolonged hypogonadism) | Asymptomatic until fragility fracture. In men, low testosterone → bone loss. | DEXA scan Z-score < -2.0, history of low-trauma fracture. | Treat hypogonadism: if prolactin normalizes, gonadal function returns. May need bisphosphonates if severe. |
| **Visual loss** (chiasmal compression) | Progressive bitemporal hemianopia → tunnel vision. Often patient unaware until advanced. | Formal perimetry showing superior temporal defect. | Urgent MRI; high-dose steroids; consider surgery if no response to agonist. |
| **Pituitary apoplexy** | Sudden severe headache, vomiting, visual loss, diplopia, hypotension (if haemorrhage). | CT head shows haemorrhagic sellar mass; hypotension may mimic meningitis. | Emergency: IV hydrocortisone (100 mg), neurosurgery, fluids. |
| **Hypopituitarism** (especially if macro) | Fatigue, weight loss, cold intolerance, loss of body hair. | Low cortisol, low TSH +/- low T4, low GH. | Replace deficient hormones; hydrocortisone first (before T4 to avoid adrenal crisis). |
| **Infertility** (undiagnosed or untreated) | Failure to conceive despite 1 year trying. | Anovulation + amenorrhea. | Cabergoline restores ovulatory cycles in 80-90% within 6 months. |
| **Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) rhinorrhoea** | Clear fluid from nose, especially after tumour shrinkage (medical or surgical). | Test for beta-2 transferrin. High risk in large macroadenomas eroding sella. | Rule out meningitis; surgical repair if persistent. |
| **Malignant prolactinoma** (very rare) | Rapid growth, high prolactin >10,000, metastases (CSF, brain, systemic). | Craniospinal MRI, biopsy. | Radiotherapy + temozolomide; poor prognosis. |

**Late-presentation patterns in exams**

- **Middle-aged woman with “tunnel vision” and history of missed periods 10 years ago** – classic miss. Look for optic atrophy and bitemporal hemianopia on exam.
- **Man with erectile dysfunction, low testosterone, and non-healing wrist fracture** – prolactinoma causing hypogonadism and osteoporosis.
- **Postmenopausal woman with incidental pituitary mass on CT head for syncope** – prolactin level decides if it’s prolactinoma or non-functioning.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics**

1. **“GAPS” for prolactinoma complications**  
   - **G** – Galactorrhea,  
   - **A** – Amenorrhea,  
   - **P** – Prolactin elevation,  
   - **S** – Size on MRI (micro vs macro)

2. **“CLEAR” for causes of hyperprolactinemia**  
   - **C** – Compression (stalk effect)  
   - **L** – Lactogenic (pregnancy, nipple stimulation)  
   - **E** – Endocrine (hypothyroidism, renal failure)  
   - **A** – Adenoma (prolactinoma)  
   - **R** – Rest (drugs, chest wall)

3. **“D2 PREEN” for drugs causing hyperprolactinemia**  
   - D2 – Dopamine antagonist  
   - P – Phenothiazines (haloperidol)  
   - R – Risperidone  
   - E – Escitalopram (SSRIs mild)  
   - E – Emetic drugs (metoclopramide, domperidone)  
   - N – Neuroleptics (other antipsychotics)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues)**

- **Prolactin >500 → macroprolactinoma** until proven otherwise. Even if MRI shows micro, repeat with contrast looking for stalk.
- **Prolactin <200 in a man with mass** → think non-functioning adenoma causing stalk effect, NOT prolactinoma. Dopamine agonist will not shrink this mass.
- **If prolactin is very high (>10,000)**, suspect aggressive/malignant variant. Urgent MRI brain and spine; need radiotherapy.
- **Normalization of prolactin with cabergoline does NOT always mean tumor shrinkage** – repeat MRI at 6 months.
- **Check prolactin BEFORE any pituitary stimulation test** – if you give TRH, you will artificially elevate it.
- **Always rule out pregnancy** before starting dopamine agonist (routinely ask about LMP, perform β-hCG).
- **Impulse control disorders** (gambling, hypersexuality) can occur with dopamine agonists – warn patient and family.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Not checking macroprolactin in asymptomatic patient – leads to unnecessary lifelong medication.
- Starting cabergoline without checking TSH/free T4 – missed hypothyroidism treatable with levothyroxine alone.
- Forgetting to ask about antipsychotics – exam classic: patient on haloperidol, prolactin 150, but you start cabergoline, making psychotic symptoms worse.
- Assuming MRI negative means no prolactinoma – microprolactinomas <3 mm may be missed; treat clinically if prolactin persistently >300.
- Not monitoring visual fields for macroprolactinoma – patient may become blind while on treatment.
- Stopping cabergoline abruptly in a pregnant woman – safe to continue until pregnancy confirmed, then stop; but if symptoms of tumour enlargement occur (headache, visual change), restart and monitor closely.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| When to escalate | Trigger | Immediate action |
|------------------|---------|------------------|
| **Pituitary apoplexy** | Sudden severe headache + vomiting + visual loss + hypotension | **Call ICU / neurosurgery immediately.** Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg, fluids, CT head. |
| **Rapid visual deterioration** (over hours) | New or worsening bitemporal hemianopia, or acuity drop | **Urgent MRI + ophthalmology review.** Start IV dexamethasone 4 mg. If no response to medical treatment, surgical decompression within 24-48h. |
| **CSF rhinorrhoea** | Clear nasal discharge + headache, fever | **Call neurosurgery.** Test fluid for beta-2 transferrin. Start IV ceftriaxone if meningitis suspected. |
| **Drug-induced psychosis/impulse control disorder** | Gambling, hypersexuality, aggression on cabergoline | **Reduce or stop cabergoline** (taper). Refer to psychiatry. |
| **Acute adrenal insufficiency (if on steroids or hypopituitarism)** | Hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycemia, vomiting | **Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, then 50 mg q6h. ICU if shock.** Check cortisol. |
| **NEWS2 >5** | Any trigger (infection, surgery, apoplexy) | Escalate to medical registrar. Consider steroid cover if on hydrocortisone. |
| **GCS <15 or new focal neurological deficit** | Likely apoplexy or tumour enlargement | **CT head stat, call neurosurgery, ICU referral.** |
| **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (if apoplexy with haemorrhage) | Shock, acidosis | **Goal-directed fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion if needed, ICU.** |

**Threshold for ICU admission:** Any patient with suspected pituitary apoplexy, GCS <15, haemodynamic instability, or need for ventilation. Also consider for those with severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) from hypocortisolism.

**When to call a senior:**
- Any **new visual symptom** – do not wait for formal perimetry.
- **Prolactin >10,000** – rare, may be malignant.
- **No response to cabergoline** after 3 months (resistance) – need surgical/endocrine MDT.
- **Pregnancy + macroprolactinoma** – high risk of tumour growth; need monthly visual fields and prolactin; endocrinology and obstetric consultation.

rawMarkdown:
## Prolactinoma

**One-liner:** Benign pituitary lactotroph adenoma causing hyperprolactinemia → hypogonadism, galactorrhea, and mass effects; commonest pituitary tumour, readily treatable.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette**
A 28-year-old woman presents with 6 months of secondary amenorrhoea (previously regular cycles). She mentions spontaneous milky discharge from both breasts when she squeezes them, and she and her partner have been trying for a baby for over a year. She has no significant past medical history, takes no regular drugs (specifically not antipsychotics or antiemetics) and has normal thyroid function. She does not complain of headache or visual disturbance. On examination, BMI is 27 kg/m² and there is bilateral galactorrhea expressible but no other focal neurology.

**Dissect the vignette – pattern clues**

| Clue | What it tells you |
|------|-------------------|
| Secondary amenorrhoea | Hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis disturbance → think hyperprolactinemia as top cause in young woman |
| Bilateral galactorrhea | Prolactin directly stimulates breast tissue; non-puerperal = pathological unless nipple stimulation |
| Infertility | Disrupted GnRH pulsatility → anovulation |
| No drug history | Rules out dopamine-antagonist induced hyperprolactinemia |
| Normal TSH | Rules out hypothyroidism as cause |
| No headache/visual symptoms | Suggests microadenoma (<1 cm) – macroadenomas often present with mass effect |
| Age/sex | Women of reproductive age = peak incidence; microadenomas more common in women |

**Atypical presentations that may trick you**

- **Postmenopausal woman** – Found on incidental brain imaging (e.g., for dizziness). No menstrual clue. Galactorrhea may be absent because oestrogen ‘priming’ is low. May present with mass effect (bitemporal hemianopia) or hypopituitarism.
- **Man with erectile dysfunction and low libido** – Often underdiagnosed because men rarely check for galactorrhea. Prolactin suppresses LH/FSH → hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. Testosterone low, SHBG low, prolactin high.
- **Child with headache and delayed puberty** – Rare, but consider if growth arrest + visual field defect. Prolactin may be only mildly elevated unless large tumour.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Prolactinoma is a benign (WHO grade I) adenoma arising from lactotroph cells of the anterior pituitary, secreting prolactin in excess. It ranges from microadenomas (<1 cm, usually intrasellar) to macroadenomas (≥1 cm, possibly suprasellar extension).

**Why it matters clinically**

- **Most common pituitary adenoma** (40-60% of all) – incidence ~10 per million per year.
- **Highly treatable** with dopamine agonists; response rates >90% for microprolactinomas. Surgery rarely needed.
- **Missed leads to preventable morbidity** – infertility, osteoporosis due to prolonged hypogonadism, and in macroadenomas: visual loss (irreversible), hypopituitarism, and rarely pituitary apoplexy.
- **Common pitfall:** assuming all hyperprolactinemia is adenoma – up to 40% is drug-induced, hypothyroid, or due to stalk compression from non-prolactin-secreting mass.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

The story begins in the hypothalamus. Dopamine, released from tuberoinfundibular neurons, travels via the hypothalamic-pituitary portal system to the anterior pituitary, where it binds D2 receptors on lactotrophs and **tonically inhibits** prolactin release. Prolactinoma cells lose this inhibitory regulation because they are monoclonal neoplasms with altered D2-receptor signalling.

**Key mechanism → sign chain**

| Mechanism | Resulting sign |
|-----------|----------------|
| Excess prolactin → inhibition of GnRH pulsatility | **Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism** – amenorrhoea, anovulation, erectile dysfunction |
| Loss of GnRH → low LH/FSH → low oestrogen/testosterone | **Osteoporosis** (chronic), **infertility**, **loss of libido** |
| Direct stimulatory effect of prolactin on breast alveolar epithelium | **Galactorrhea** (requires oestrogen priming – more common in premenopausal women) |
| Enlarging adenoma pushes against normal pituitary tissue | **Hypopituitarism** (especially GH, gonadotropins first) |
| Upward growth compresses optic chiasm (most commonly crossing fibres from nasal retinae) | **Bitemporal hemianopia** (early sign: superior quadrantanopia) |
| Lateral extension into cavernous sinus | **Cranial nerve palsies** (III, IV, VI, V1 – rare unless aggressive) |
| Stalk compression by tumour (even if non-secreting) interrupts dopamine flow | **Stalk effect** – hyperprolactinemia from loss of tonic inhibition, often mild (<200 ng/mL) |

**Why pattern recognition matters:** A woman with amenorrhea and galactorrhea – prolactinoma is likely if prolactin >200 ng/mL (tumour) vs <200 ng/mL (stalk effect or other cause). The tumour size loosely correlates with prolactin level: micros usually 200-500, macros >500-1000. Very high levels (>5000) suggest giant or aggressive prolactinoma or malignant transformation.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

**Bin 1 – Idiopathic / spontaneous**
- Most common (~60% of all hyperprolactinemia is not a tumour – stress, nipple stimulation, exercise, sleep, pregnancy). For prolactinoma specifically, ~90% are sporadic.
- **Stem clue:** young woman, no drugs, normal thyroid, prolactin 50-200 ng/mL → often idiopathic hyperprolactinemia (may still receive low-dose agonist if symptomatic).

**Bin 2 – Pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma)**
- Micro (<1 cm) vs macro (≥1 cm)
- **Stem clue:** prolactin >200 ng/mL; degree correlates with tumour size. No other pituitary abnormality on MRI.

**Bin 3 – Drug-induced (functional hyperprolactinemia)**
- **Antipsychotics** (typical >> atypical, but risperidone/paliperidone high risk) – block D2 receptors.
- **Antiemetics** – metoclopramide, domperidone (dopamine antagonists).
- **Others**: verapamil, methyldopa, oral contraceptives (weak), SSRIs (mild).
- **Stem clue:** recent medication start. Withdrawal normalizes prolactin within days-weeks.

**Bin 4 – Hypothalamic-pituitary stalk disorders (“disconnection hyperprolactinemia”)**
- *Stalk compression* by non-prolactin-secreting pituitary mass (non-functioning adenoma, craniopharyngioma, meningioma, Rathke’s cleft cyst) or granulomatous disease (sarcoidosis, histiocytosis)
- **Stem clue:** prolactin <200 ng/mL (usually <150); mass on MRI not arising from sella alone.

**Bin 5 – Hypothyroidism**
- Loss of negative feedback → TRH increase → TRH stimulates lactotrophs as well as thyrotrophs.
- **Stem clue:** elevated TSH, low free T4, prolactin mildly elevated. Replacement of thyroxine normalizes prolactin.

**Bin 6 – Renal failure**
- Decreased clearance of prolactin.
- **Stem clue:** Cr >300, on dialysis. Prolactin usually <200.

**Bin 7 – Miscellaneous**
- Chest wall trauma/surgery/shingles (neural reflex), nipple piercing.
- **Stem clue:** history of chest wall irritation, prolactin mildly high.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria (no formal score – clinical + lab + imaging)**
- **Confirmed hyperprolactinemia** on at least two occasions (>30 ng/mL in women, >20 ng/mL in men) after excluding drugs, hypothyroidism, renal failure, pregnancy.
- **MRI pituitary with contrast** showing a pituitary adenoma (micro or macro).
- **Visual field perimetry** if macroadenoma abutting chiasm.

**Individual tests**

| Test | What it shows | Why order | How to interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Serum prolactin** | Direct measure | First line | >200 ng/mL strongly suggests prolactinoma; >500 is diagnostic of macroprolactinoma. If very high (>10,000) suspect aggressive/malignant. |
| **Macroprolactin screen** (PEG precipitation) | Detects macroprolactin (big prolactin-immunoglobulin complex) – biologically inactive | If prolactin elevated but patient has no symptoms of hyperprolactinemia (especially men/older women) | True prolactin <30% of total = macroprolactinemia (no treatment needed) |
| **TSH, free T4** | Thyroid function | Exclude hypothyroidism as cause | Elevated TSH + low free T4 → primary hypothyroidism |
| **LH, FSH, oestradiol/testosterone** | Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism | Confirm gonadal insufficiency | Low LH/FSH + low sex steroids |
| **MRI pituitary (sella protocol)** | Tumour size, extent, stalk, chiasm, cavernous sinus | Gold standard imaging | Micro (<1 cm) vs macro (≥1 cm). Look for cystic components, haemorrhage (apoplexy). |
| **Visual field perimetry** | Visual field defects (early superior bitemporal quadrantanopia) | If macroadenoma or any visual complaint | Repeat every 6-12 months until stable |
| **Pituitary function panel** (cortisol, ACTH, IGF-1, etc.) | Rule out hypopituitarism | Especially if macro; baseline before treatment | If cortisol low → needs hydrocortisone; GH deficiency may not need replacement |

**Single finding that confirms / rules out**

- **Confirms macroprolactinoma:** MRI shows ≥1 cm pituitary tumour + prolactin >500 ng/mL.
- **Rules out prolactinoma:** Normal MRI with prolactin <200 ng/mL and no other cause (then idiopathic hyperprolactinemia or microprolactinoma if MRI not sensitive). Very low prolactin (<100) in presence of pituitary mass = likely stalk effect (non-prolactinoma mass).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – if visual compromise or apoplexy**
- **What:** Urgent MRI, ophthalmology review, start high-dose dexamethasone 4 mg IV stat (to reduce oedema) and arrange neurosurgical consultation.
- **Why:** Rapid tumor expansion can cause irreversible vision loss if not decompressed urgently.
- **NOT:** Do not start dopamine agonist if there is apoplexy with hypotension (risk of vasodilation). First, stabilize haemodynamics, give steroids, then plan surgery.

**Ward (24-72 hours) – for stable patients**

- **First-line medical therapy:** **Cabergoline** – a long-acting dopamine D2-receptor agonist.
  - Dose: 0.25-0.5 mg twice weekly orally (start 0.25 mg twice weekly, increase after 4 weeks based on prolactin).
  - Why: Normalizes prolactin in >90%, shrinks tumours, less GI side effects than bromocriptine.
  - Monitor prolactin monthly until normal, then every 6-12 months. MRI at 6 months to assess tumour shrinkage.
- **Second-line:** **Bromocriptine** 2.5-5 mg daily (start 1.25 mg at bedtime, increase over weeks) – only if cabergoline not tolerated or unavailable.
- **Supportive:** Reassure patient, discuss side effects (nausea, postural hypotension, dizziness, rarely impulse control disorders). Give with food, take at bedtime.

- **Check:** Baseline prolactin, MRI, visual fields if macroadenoma. Rule out pregnancy (β-hCG) before starting agonist.

- **What NOT to do:** Don’t give dopamine agonists to patients with psychogenic causes (e.g., antipsychotic-induced) unless symptomatic – usually withdraw the offending drug if safe. Don’t start agonist without confirming no macroprolactinemia.

**Long-term (months-years)**

- **Goal:** Normal prolactin, restored gonadal function, tumour shrinkage.
- **Maintenance cabergoline:** Continue at lowest effective dose. Re-evaluate every 6-12 months.
- **Taper after 2-3 years** if prolactin remains normal and MRI shows no tumour. Attempt to stop drug entirely in 1 in 20 patients (success depends on size, original prolactin). Resumes if prolactin rises.
- **Surgery (transsphenoidal adenomectomy):** Reserved for:
  - Drug intolerance or resistance (failure to normalize prolactin after maximum dose 3 months)
  - Macroprolactinoma with mass effect not responding
  - Apoplexy or CSF leak
  - Cystic prolactinomas (surgery better)
- **Radiotherapy:** Rare – only for aggressive, malignant, or recurrent tumors after surgery and drug failure.

**Common pitfalls**

- **Not checking macroprolactin** in asymptomatic patients – leads to unnecessary treatment.
- **Starting agonist without checking TSH/free T4** – missed hypothyroidism.
- **Not asking about antipsychotics** – classic exam trick.
- **Assuming all pituitary mass is prolactinoma** – stalk effect from non-functioning adenoma will have mildly elevated prolactin (<200) and no tumour shrinkage with dopamine agonist. If prolactin does not normalize or mass does not shrink, consider biopsy.
- **Forgetting pregnancy counseling** – cabergoline is safe in early pregnancy, but stop as soon as pregnancy confirmed; monitor for tumour enlargement (risk 3% for micro, 30% for macro).

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Prolactinoma | Hypothyroidism (primary) | Drug-induced | Stalk effect (non-functioning mass) |
|---------|--------------|--------------------------|--------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Prolactin** | >200 ng/mL (micro), >500 (macro) | Mildly elevated (<200) | Mild-mod elevated (<200) | Mildly elevated (<200) |
| **TSH** | Normal | High | Normal | Normal |
| **Free T4** | Normal | Low | Normal | Normal |
| **LH/FSH** | Low (hypogonadotropic) | Normal or low | Variable | Low if mass compresses gonadotrophs |
| **Galactorrhea** | Common (if premenopausal) | Uncommon | Possible | Rare |
| **MRI** | Sellar adenoma (micro/macro) | Normal | Normal | Non-prolactin-secreting mass (stalk compression) |
| **Drug history** | None | None | Haloperidol/risperidone/metoclopramide | None |
| **Key distinguishing test** | Prolactin level + MRI | TSH + free T4 | Withdrawal of drug normalizes prolactin | MRI shows stalk deviation; prolactin not >200 |
| **Management** | Dopamine agonist | Levothyroxine | Stop/replace drug | Surgery if mass effect; dopamine agonist won’t shrink mass |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**If untreated or diagnosed late**

| Complication | Presentation | Key signs | What to do |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|------------|
| **Osteoporosis** (due to prolonged hypogonadism) | Asymptomatic until fragility fracture. In men, low testosterone → bone loss. | DEXA scan Z-score < -2.0, history of low-trauma fracture. | Treat hypogonadism: if prolactin normalizes, gonadal function returns. May need bisphosphonates if severe. |
| **Visual loss** (chiasmal compression) | Progressive bitemporal hemianopia → tunnel vision. Often patient unaware until advanced. | Formal perimetry showing superior temporal defect. | Urgent MRI; high-dose steroids; consider surgery if no response to agonist. |
| **Pituitary apoplexy** | Sudden severe headache, vomiting, visual loss, diplopia, hypotension (if haemorrhage). | CT head shows haemorrhagic sellar mass; hypotension may mimic meningitis. | Emergency: IV hydrocortisone (100 mg), neurosurgery, fluids. |
| **Hypopituitarism** (especially if macro) | Fatigue, weight loss, cold intolerance, loss of body hair. | Low cortisol, low TSH +/- low T4, low GH. | Replace deficient hormones; hydrocortisone first (before T4 to avoid adrenal crisis). |
| **Infertility** (undiagnosed or untreated) | Failure to conceive despite 1 year trying. | Anovulation + amenorrhea. | Cabergoline restores ovulatory cycles in 80-90% within 6 months. |
| **Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) rhinorrhoea** | Clear fluid from nose, especially after tumour shrinkage (medical or surgical). | Test for beta-2 transferrin. High risk in large macroadenomas eroding sella. | Rule out meningitis; surgical repair if persistent. |
| **Malignant prolactinoma** (very rare) | Rapid growth, high prolactin >10,000, metastases (CSF, brain, systemic). | Craniospinal MRI, biopsy. | Radiotherapy + temozolomide; poor prognosis. |

**Late-presentation patterns in exams**

- **Middle-aged woman with “tunnel vision” and history of missed periods 10 years ago** – classic miss. Look for optic atrophy and bitemporal hemianopia on exam.
- **Man with erectile dysfunction, low testosterone, and non-healing wrist fracture** – prolactinoma causing hypogonadism and osteoporosis.
- **Postmenopausal woman with incidental pituitary mass on CT head for syncope** – prolactin level decides if it’s prolactinoma or non-functioning.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics**

1. **“GAPS” for prolactinoma complications**  
   - **G** – Galactorrhea,  
   - **A** – Amenorrhea,  
   - **P** – Prolactin elevation,  
   - **S** – Size on MRI (micro vs macro)

2. **“CLEAR” for causes of hyperprolactinemia**  
   - **C** – Compression (stalk effect)  
   - **L** – Lactogenic (pregnancy, nipple stimulation)  
   - **E** – Endocrine (hypothyroidism, renal failure)  
   - **A** – Adenoma (prolactinoma)  
   - **R** – Rest (drugs, chest wall)

3. **“D2 PREEN” for drugs causing hyperprolactinemia**  
   - D2 – Dopamine antagonist  
   - P – Phenothiazines (haloperidol)  
   - R – Risperidone  
   - E – Escitalopram (SSRIs mild)  
   - E – Emetic drugs (metoclopramide, domperidone)  
   - N – Neuroleptics (other antipsychotics)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues)**

- **Prolactin >500 → macroprolactinoma** until proven otherwise. Even if MRI shows micro, repeat with contrast looking for stalk.
- **Prolactin <200 in a man with mass** → think non-functioning adenoma causing stalk effect, NOT prolactinoma. Dopamine agonist will not shrink this mass.
- **If prolactin is very high (>10,000)**, suspect aggressive/malignant variant. Urgent MRI brain and spine; need radiotherapy.
- **Normalization of prolactin with cabergoline does NOT always mean tumor shrinkage** – repeat MRI at 6 months.
- **Check prolactin BEFORE any pituitary stimulation test** – if you give TRH, you will artificially elevate it.
- **Always rule out pregnancy** before starting dopamine agonist (routinely ask about LMP, perform β-hCG).
- **Impulse control disorders** (gambling, hypersexuality) can occur with dopamine agonists – warn patient and family.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid**

- Not checking macroprolactin in asymptomatic patient – leads to unnecessary lifelong medication.
- Starting cabergoline without checking TSH/free T4 – missed hypothyroidism treatable with levothyroxine alone.
- Forgetting to ask about antipsychotics – exam classic: patient on haloperidol, prolactin 150, but you start cabergoline, making psychotic symptoms worse.
- Assuming MRI negative means no prolactinoma – microprolactinomas <3 mm may be missed; treat clinically if prolactin persistently >300.
- Not monitoring visual fields for macroprolactinoma – patient may become blind while on treatment.
- Stopping cabergoline abruptly in a pregnant woman – safe to continue until pregnancy confirmed, then stop; but if symptoms of tumour enlargement occur (headache, visual change), restart and monitor closely.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| When to escalate | Trigger | Immediate action |
|------------------|---------|------------------|
| **Pituitary apoplexy** | Sudden severe headache + vomiting + visual loss + hypotension | **Call ICU / neurosurgery immediately.** Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg, fluids, CT head. |
| **Rapid visual deterioration** (over hours) | New or worsening bitemporal hemianopia, or acuity drop | **Urgent MRI + ophthalmology review.** Start IV dexamethasone 4 mg. If no response to medical treatment, surgical decompression within 24-48h. |
| **CSF rhinorrhoea** | Clear nasal discharge + headache, fever | **Call neurosurgery.** Test fluid for beta-2 transferrin. Start IV ceftriaxone if meningitis suspected. |
| **Drug-induced psychosis/impulse control disorder** | Gambling, hypersexuality, aggression on cabergoline | **Reduce or stop cabergoline** (taper). Refer to psychiatry. |
| **Acute adrenal insufficiency (if on steroids or hypopituitarism)** | Hypotension, hyponatraemia, hypoglycemia, vomiting | **Give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat, then 50 mg q6h. ICU if shock.** Check cortisol. |
| **NEWS2 >5** | Any trigger (infection, surgery, apoplexy) | Escalate to medical registrar. Consider steroid cover if on hydrocortisone. |
| **GCS <15 or new focal neurological deficit** | Likely apoplexy or tumour enlargement | **CT head stat, call neurosurgery, ICU referral.** |
| **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (if apoplexy with haemorrhage) | Shock, acidosis | **Goal-directed fluid resuscitation, blood transfusion if needed, ICU.** |

**Threshold for ICU admission:** Any patient with suspected pituitary apoplexy, GCS <15, haemodynamic instability, or need for ventilation. Also consider for those with severe hyponatraemia (Na <120) from hypocortisolism.

**When to call a senior:**
- Any **new visual symptom** – do not wait for formal perimetry.
- **Prolactin >10,000** – rare, may be malignant.
- **No response to cabergoline** after 3 months (resistance) – need surgical/endocrine MDT.
- **Pregnancy + macroprolactinoma** – high risk of tumour growth; need monthly visual fields and prolactin; endocrinology and obstetric consultation.
